entries.markdown:
THE AUDITION NOTES:
THE TONE — Brisk-tender. Hands that know the work; the care arrives as a thing DONE, never a thing said.
WHY THIS WAY — This reader rehearses, over-explains, braces to be asked for a performance — treats every room as a test they could fail. So the deck asks for nothing: the work is plain and observable, and every kindness is an action that needs no reply, so there is nothing here to get wrong. The BELL, not the reader, ends the watch — finishing-imperfect is built into the world's clock, which lets the one who can never call a thing "done" off the hook by something outside themselves. The risk in brisk is cold; the guard against it is that the warmth lives in the doing (the cup, the kept bunk) and the log says outright what the deck never will.
THE CHOICES —
1. **Care as ACTION, not speech** — the cup set down and walked away from; the line already flaked for your hands. Warmth you can't be expected to answer.
2. **The bell ends the watch** — the clock decides, not you; staged the first night so the rule lands early.
3. **No name, no reason asked** — no one requests either; the manifest logs you anyway, at true weight, including the night you only carried yourself.
PRIMER TOUCHPOINTS — what this pilot is a worked example of (`storybuilding-primer.md`): §3 WITNESS-NOT-FIXER + holding-space (care as a thing done, no reply asked) and the gift-at-REL-0 (the cup, night one); "logged at full weight" = felt-understanding · §2.5 objects-as-subtext (the tin cup; the blank column left "the way you'd leave a chair") · §3.7 the subtext gap ("Hold the rail, not the rope. Rope's got somewhere to be." — care, never named) · §3.5 the cast as role-monikers + the un-imposed name (no name asked — yours to keep) · §3.6 one face foregrounded (the Deckhand), the rest glimpsed (Cook · Pilot · Keeper) · §2 the Chronicler narrator (the manifest, entered after) · §5 no-fail-punish (the bell ends the watch, done or not).
---
*From the manifest, CROSSING 1 — entered after, in the same ink as every other line: lamps lit at the usual hour; one aboard, no cargo declared. The new ones never know the bunk's already made.*

The pier had one lamp and a slate with the night's word chalked on it — CALM WATER. Last night's was already wiped clean.

The DECKHAND was at the rail, flaking a line into a coil that would pay out without a snag. He didn't look up until your shoe found the gangway.

"Hold the rail, not the rope," he said. "Rope's got somewhere to be."

You held the rail. He waited the half-second it took your weight to commit — then threw the lever. The ramp came up behind you with a sound like a held breath let go. You were aboard before you'd quite decided to be.

He nodded once, the way you'd nod at a job done right, and went back to the line.

An amber square opened down the deck — the galley. The COOK set a tin cup on the rail beside you, steam coming off it, and was gone before you found a word. Cocoa, near enough. You hadn't asked. No one had asked you anything.

At the wheel, half-seen, the PILOT thumbed the compass a degree and held it there — a motion worn smooth by ten thousand nights. The strait ahead was all dark. She steered it like a room she knew with the lights off.

A bell rang once, forward. Not loud. In the cabin light the KEEPER drew a line in a long book and left the column beside it blank — the way you'd leave a chair.

"That's the watch," the Deckhand said, to no one in particular. "Done's done when the bell says. Not before."

The shore slid back. Nobody asked you to do anything about it.

*The keeper's hand, last, in the same ink as the cargo that mattered: Stood the rail the whole way. Carried only themselves. Logged at full weight.*

entries.markdown:
THE AUDITION NOTES:
THE TONE — Lyrical-hush. The quiet hum after the day's ringing — a held breath let go into the dark; a space, not a voice.
WHY THIS WAY — The one this is for comes to the water carrying the day's noise. They don't need a story that tries to shout over it; they need a quiet room for the ringing to fade. This Lyrical-Hush tone is that room. It makes no demands. For the one who rehearses every word, it offers a silence they don't have to fill. For the one who carries more than they admit, it describes the cold of the rail and the weight of the dark, letting the world echo what they feel without asking them to name it. The risk is slowness; the promise is stillness.
THE CHOICES — 
1.  **The Unspoken Welcome.** The first interaction is non-verbal. The Deckhand doesn't ask for a ticket or a name; he sees you, acknowledges you with a slight tip of his chin, and does his work. His competence is the welcome, respecting the player's exhaustion and reluctance to perform.
2.  **The World Breathes First.** The player takes one action: stepping aboard. The world does the rest. The Deckhand raises the gangway, the engine hums to life, the lamps light themselves. This externalizes initiation, lifting the burden of *starting* from the one who feels they cannot begin.
3.  **Objects as History.** The story is in the ferry's worn surfaces. The Deckhand's rope isn't just a rope; its differently-colored whipping is a quiet testament to decades of service, a story the player can see but doesn't have to decode. The world has history, and is stable.
PRIMER TOUCHPOINTS — 
§1 The Open Question (a quiet hook): The tri-colored whipping on the mooring line; the reason for the crossing itself.
§2 The Chronicler Firewall: The italicized log entries are the Keeper's retrospective record, narrating the unseen meaning ("the carry was the passenger themselves"), not live action.
§2.5 Environmental Storytelling: The Deckhand's mooring line, with its end whipped in three different colors, suggests long history without exposition.
§3 Holding Space / Gift at REL 0: The ferry crossing itself is the gift—a contained hour that asks nothing. The crew's silent presence holds space without trying to fix.
§3.6 One New Face at a Time: The Deckhand is the only foregrounded character; the Cook, Pilot, and Keeper are brief, establishing glimpses in the periphery.
§5 Cozy Framework / Contained World: The ferry deck becomes a small, safely-lit island in the dark—a bounded, low-ambush loop that is the core of the soothing experience.
---
*The manifest would record it later, in the Keeper’s dry script: the tide, the turn of the wind, and one passenger. The space for cargo was left blank; the carry was the passenger themselves.*

The air at the dock is heavy with salt and the day you left behind. A single lamp throws a pale, tired circle on the wet planks. You stand at its edge.

The ferry waits, a dark shape against a darker sky. It makes no sound.

Your feet find the gangway. It’s just wood. It holds. You don't look back at the shore.

On deck, a man is coiling a heavy mooring line, his movements practiced and sure. The Deckhand. He doesn’t look up, but he knows you are there. You see the end of the rope in his hands, its fibers bound tight with waxed thread of three different, faded colors. A story told in knots.

He finishes, lays the coil in its place, and only then does he meet your eye. A slight tip of his chin. It is enough. He moves to the iron crank, and the chain rattles, loud in the quiet. The gangway lifts. A foot of black water opens between the ferry and the land.

The choice is gone. There is only the crossing now.

A low thrum starts deep inside the boat. Through a salt-grimed window, you glimpse a figure moving in the galley’s warm light. Another, a silhouette at the wheel, gazes out into the starless night.

The deck lamps flicker on, one by one, casting a soft, amber glow that doesn't touch the water. You find a place at the rail. The wood is cold, worn smooth by a thousand other hands. The shore is a memory. The air is clean.

*He didn't write that the passenger finally let their shoulders fall, or that the quiet felt like a kindness. The log has no column for such things. It only records that the crossing was made, and that was the part that counted.*

The pilot perfectly establishes the Deckhand, Pilot, and Manifest-Keeper by role and in character, but the Cook is only a brief glimpse. While present as a 'figure moving in the galley’s warm light,' their specific 'blunt-fond' personality from the concept is not yet established.

The pilot carries the motif perfectly, using the log/manifest as a framing device and explicitly centering the narrative on 'the crossing.' The 'blank column' is also present in the log's omissions, such as when 'The space for cargo was left blank.'

The pilot perfectly establishes the Manifest-Keeper's log as a retrospective, third-person narrator. It nails the core concept by contrasting the log's 'dry script' with the unrecorded emotional details, noting that 'The log has no column for such things'.

entries.markdown:
THE AUDITION NOTES:
THE TONE — Warm-wry. Come in out of the weather — the kettle's on, the tea's in the tin; nobody's going to ask why you're here.
WHY THIS WAY — This reader arrives braced, expecting to be asked for something they don't have—a reason, a plan, a cheerful face. The WARM-WRY tone is a quiet refusal to demand anything. It offers presence without pressure, a form of respect for the player's exhaustion. Its deadpan warmth is a shield against the kind of forced sentimentality that would make a guarded person retreat. It doesn't offer a hand to hold; it points to a dry seat out of the wind and trusts you'll take it when you're ready.
THE CHOICES — 
1. **A Welcome of Fact, not Feeling:** The Deckhand doesn't say "welcome," he says "Tide's turning." It's an observation about the world that doubles as an instruction, externalizing the need for a decision and making the act of boarding a simple response to a natural rhythm, not a personal trial.
2. **The Hook is Held, Not Shown:** The central mystery isn't a prepared absence (like an empty bunk), but a present, personal object—the stone the Deckhand carries. It makes the hook about a person's inner world, not just the ship's architecture, promising a story of character over plot.
3. **The Narrator as Witness to the Unspoken:** The Keeper's log frames the crossing by recording the subtext of the night—noting the player's hesitation on the pier and the ambiguity in the Deckhand's parting words. It establishes the narrator's role as the one who sees the internal weight, not just the external action.
PRIMER TOUCHPOINTS — 
- §1 The Open Question: The smooth, grey stone the Deckhand palms but never throws is a tangible question planted in the first scene.
- §2 The Chronicler Firewall / Narrator: The opening and closing log entries are retrospective, third-person records ("Stood the pier awhile... Logged at full weight"), narrating the unseen meaning, not the seen action.
- §2.5 Environmental Storytelling: The brass rail worn smooth by hands, the smell of stock from the galley—the ship's life is told through sensory details of its routine.
- §3 Gift at REL 0 / Holding Space: The crossing itself is the gift. The crew's non-interference is the form their care takes; the Deckhand watches you board, but doesn't rush or "help" you.
- §3.6 One new face at a time: The Deckhand is the only crew member you speak to; the Cook and Pilot are present only as sensory traces (a smell, a silhouette), staging the cast reveal slowly.
- §3.7 The Subtext Gap: The Deckhand’s line, "That's the hard part done," is practical on its surface but carries an unspoken understanding of the effort it took just to get on board.
---
*The manifest keeper’s entry for the night is spare, as always. Stood the pier awhile. Watched the gulls settle. Boarded before the tide turned. Logged at full weight, as is only proper.*

The air had gone cold, smelling of salt and rain on stone. At the pier's end, the ferry idled, its engine a steady heartbeat in the dark. It wasn't a vessel for long journeys; it was squat, solid, and looked like it had been repainted a dozen times, always the same colour.

The Deckhand was leaning against a railing, polishing a small, smooth, grey stone with his thumb. He looked up as you got closer, his face weathered into something that wasn't quite a smile but wasn't a frown either. He didn't ask what you wanted. He just pocketed the stone and nodded at the gangway. "Tide's turning," he said, his voice like gravel rolling in a quiet place. "So's the gangway."

You stepped on. The metal was cold through the soles of your shoes. From an open galley vent came the warm, savoury smell of simmering stock; through the doorway the little table was laid for four, and a fifth place at the end, its bowl turned down — kept for no one in particular. Ahead, you could see the dark shape of the Pilot in the wheelhouse, a still silhouette against the soft green glow of the instruments. You put a hand on the brass rail leading to the passenger cabin; it was worn smooth, cool and solid from a thousand other hands.

The Deckhand pulled a lever. With a low hydraulic hiss, the gangway lifted and swung away, leaving a strip of churning black water between you and the shore.

"Right," he said, turning to check a knot on a nearby line. "That's the hard part done." He didn't look at you when he said it.

*The log does not record which hard part he meant. The crossing is the same length for everyone. The getting on board is not.*

reviews.why: For someone whose biggest struggle is internal—the decision to participate, to be present, to take up space—having that internal struggle acknowledged by an outsider is incredibly validating. It frames their biggest effort as the real accomplishment.

reviews.author: The one who rehearses

reviews.author: The one who carries it all

When the kind act is done without eye contact, it becomes less of a direct gift and more of a neutral observation they can accept without feeling indebted.
reviews.act: THE KEEP — The casual, indirect delivery. The kindness is offered without demanding a witness or a receipt. It's a gift that can be pocketed without thanks.

reviews.author: The one who can't call it done

reviews.author: The one who rehearses

reviews.act: THE KEEP — The lack of eye contact. It turns a potentially transactional moment of comfort into a quiet, un-demanding observation that the character can simply absorb without having to respond.

moments.anchor: Logged at full weight, as is only proper.

reviews.author: The one who carries it all

The pilot establishes the night ferry, its crew, and the somber crossing, but it drifts slightly from the concept. The pilot carries the cast element perfectly, introducing all four crew members by their roles and establishing their core traits. The Manifest-Keeper’s log is perfectly 'dry-precise,' and the Pilot is a 'still silhouette,' exactly as sketched.

The pilot successfully integrates all aspects of the motif, opening with the log entry, establishing the crossing as the central event, and explicitly showing the 'fifth place at the end, its bowl turned down'.

The pilot perfectly establishes the Manifest-Keeper's log as a retrospective, third-person chronicler, fulfilling the element. The log's spare, observational tone, as in "The getting on board is not," correctly contrasts with the unstated warmth found in the main narrative.

entries.markdown:
THE AUDITION NOTES:
THE TONE — Folk-mythic. An old, patient rite: the crossing has carried thousands before, and there's rest in being one of the long line.
WHY THIS WAY — This reader arrives sure their trouble is singular — too heavy, too odd, too much to ask of anyone. A folk-mythic tone answers that without a word of comfort: the ferry has made this crossing ten thousand times and carried everyone, so nothing they bring is new, too heavy, or out of order. For the one who can't start, the world already knows the steps. For the one who carries too much, the boat has carried heavier. For the one who rehearses, the rite asks nothing they have to perform. The risk is that mythic tips grand-and-cold or fairytale-twee — so the grandeur lives in the PATIENCE and the worn wood, never the prose: an old working boat, not a legend.
THE CHOICES —
1. **The long line** — the comfort isn't being special; it's being one of many the ferry has carried, so the trouble stops feeling singular.
2. **The worn ritual** — everything is done the way it's always done; the pattern carries them, so no one has to decide or perform.
3. **Nothing too much** — whatever they bring, the ferry has carried heavier; they can't overload it, can't be the wrong kind of passenger.
PRIMER TOUCHPOINTS — what this pilot is a worked example of (`storybuilding-primer.md`): §1 the quiet hook (the unspoken question — how many has it carried, and is one more all right?) · §2 the Chronicler (the log as one long unbroken column; the player is the latest line) · §2.5 objects-as-subtext (the rope spliced a hundred times; the step worn low) · §3 holding-space + the gift-at-REL-0 (the ferry asks nothing and carries them exactly as it carries everyone) · §3.5 the cast as role-titles + the un-imposed name (a line in the manifest, never asked who) · §3.6 one face foregrounded (the Deckhand), the rest glimpsed (Cook · Pilot · Keeper) · §5 the contained, repeatable world (the crossing is always the same; the safety is in the pattern).
---
*From the manifest — the same long column, ten thousand lines deep, the latest one the ferry never asked a name for: lamps lit at the usual hour. One aboard. The crossing made, as it is always made.*

They have been lighting these lamps longer than anyone aboard can say. The pier knows the hour by heart; the lamps catch one by one the way they have every night, for everyone who ever came down these steps.

You came down them the way they all come down — slower than you meant to. The DECKHAND was splicing a rope that had been spliced a hundred times, the strands worn pale where a hundred hands had held it. He has tied this knot for every crossing. He tied it for you the same.

"Mind the step," he said, not looking up. "It's worn low. They all wear it low."

You stepped where ten thousand feet had stepped, into the dip they left, and the wood took your weight like it was expecting it.

Down the deck the COOK ladled from a pot that is never empty and never the meal she planned — there has always been enough, for however many came. At the wheel the PILOT held the dark on a course older than the boat.

The KEEPER turned a page in a book grown fat with crossings and drew a fresh line under all the others. Yours. The same width as the rest.

"Long way, this," the Deckhand said, to the rope more than to you. "Ferry's carried heavier. Carried lighter. Carries it the same."

The shore let go, the way it has let go of everyone.

*The Keeper's hand, last, beneath ten thousand like it: Crossed, as they all cross. Logged at full weight, as every line is.*

moments.anchor: the wood took your weight like it was expecting it.
moments.moment: The boat was expecting your weight

reviews.author: The one who can't start
reviews.note: I like this. It feels like I'm being held without having to ask. The world made a space for me, and I just had to step into it.
reviews.why: This reader feels unable to initiate things and needs external validation to feel they belong. The boat 'expecting' their weight provides that validation passively and impersonally, without any of the social pressure or potential for rejection that comes from a person. It's a feeling of being accepted into a system without having to earn it.
reviews.act: THE KEEP — The feeling that the world itself, not a person, is making a place for the character. It's about being effortlessly incorporated into something ancient and stable.

reviews.author: The one who carries it all
reviews.note: This makes me suspicious. It feels like the world is making room for me before I've earned it, which just means I'm already in debt for the space I'm taking up.
reviews.why: A person who feels like a burden is immediately put on guard by unearned acceptance. Being 'expected' without having done anything to warrant it feels like a setup for a future demand, a bill that will come due.
reviews.act: THE FIX — Soften the active expectation to be more about the structure's history. Instead of 'like it was expecting it,' try 'and the wood held your weight like it had held all the others.'

reviews.author: The one who can't call it done
reviews.note: This makes me feel trapped, like I'm just falling into a rhythm that will never end. I'm waiting for something to happen, but this just tells me my presence changes nothing.
reviews.why: A reader who needs a clear finish line feels anxious when placed in a world that emphasizes endless, unchanging cycles. The feeling of being just one more identical part of a huge, impersonal process removes any sense of an individual journey that can be completed.
reviews.act: THE FIX — Change it to signal a slight break in the pattern: "the wood took your weight, and groaned, a sound it hadn't made for the last ten."

reviews.author: The one who rehearses
reviews.note: This is a profound relief. I feel the structure itself make a place for me, without any person having to notice me or ask me to move.
reviews.why: A person who dreads being seen or addressed finds safety in being physically accommodated without interaction. The world is making space for them, not a person who might expect a 'thank you' in return.
reviews.act: THE KEEP — The feeling that the boat itself, the inanimate object, is the one doing the welcoming. It's an acceptance that demands no social reply.

moments.moment: The ferry has carried heavier, and carries it the same

reviews.author: The one who can't start
reviews.note: This is a relief. It means my own personal baggage isn't a special problem here. I'm not too much; I'm just another passenger, and the ferry can handle it.
reviews.why: This reader is burdened by their own day and feels like an imposition. The line reassures them that their personal weight is not a unique or difficult burden for the system they've entered. It makes them feel anonymous and unjudged, which is a form of safety.
reviews.act: THE KEEP — The promise of being carried without judgment. The idea that the character's personal struggles are not a unique burden on the world around them.

reviews.author: The one who carries it all
reviews.note: This is a quiet relief. It means my personal weight, which feels so heavy to me, doesn't actually strain the system and I don't have to apologize for it.
reviews.why: For someone who constantly worries about being a burden, the idea that their specific weight doesn't matter to the larger structure is a profound comfort. It suggests they can exist without breaking things or demanding special accommodation.
reviews.act: THE KEEP — The fragile thing is the Deckhand's casual, impersonal delivery. He says it 'to the rope more than to you.' This is crucial; if he looked the character in the eye and said it with kindness, it would feel like pity and become a debt.

reviews.author: The one who can't call it done

reviews.author: The one who rehearses

reviews.why: Someone who fears being asked to perform a reason for their presence is reassured by being treated as part of an undifferentiated mass. It means they can blend in and not have their specific story investigated.
reviews.act: THE KEEP — The Deckhand's detached, impersonal delivery. He isn't talking about the passenger's feelings or burdens, but about the ferry's own long, unchanging history.

moments.anchor: The same width as the rest.

reviews.author: The one who can't start

It turns the comforting anonymity into impersonal dismissal, making them feel like an interchangeable unit rather than a person being given refuge.

reviews.author: The one who rehearses

The pilot perfectly establishes the gift of the crossing as an ancient, non-judgmental ritual that puts water between a person and their day. The Deckhand's line, "Ferry's carried heavier. Carried lighter. Carries it the same," directly states that the ferry accepts the unfinished day without it being a verdict.

The pilot creates a deeply reassuring and non-demanding space for the target reader through its calm, repetitive rhythm and explicit statements of acceptance. The Deckhand's line, 'Ferry's carried heavier. Carried lighter. Carries it the same,' directly addresses feelings of low self-worth and the need for a non-judgmental container.
The pilot introduces all four crew members by their roles as specified, and each character's brief appearance perfectly matches their locked sketch from the gruff-warm Deckhand to the precise Keeper.

The pilot fully establishes the required motifs, framing the action as a 'crossing,' centering the Keeper's log/manifest, and treating the passenger as an unnamed entry: 'the latest one the ferry never asked a name for.'

The pilot carries the element perfectly. The italicized log entries establish the specified narrator: a retrospective, third-person Chronicler voice that feels diegetic to the manifest, as in 'Crossed, as they all cross. Logged at full weight, as every line is.'

# CONCEPT — The Cloudhouse Observatory

## THE WORLD
The Cloudhouse Observatory is a soft-lit sky station moored above a ring of sleeping hills, open from first dusk until the last blue before morning. Its rooms are domes, gantries, rain-glass halls, kite decks, bell balconies, and quiet stairwells where clouds drift close enough to touch. Nothing here is in danger; the stakes are inner weather made visible. Some nights the sky is all fog, some nights every star seems to be watching, and some nights the wind will not lift a kite unless the first knot is tied anyway. Players walk in when the day has felt too loud, too blank, too crowded, or too unfinished. The Cloudhouse does not ask them to become grand. It asks them to read one patch of sky, make one mark, send one signal, and let a rough little constellation count.

## THE GIFT
At REL 0, the player receives the Weather Glass: a pocket lens that turns the night’s sky into a tiny personal forecast. It is useful before anyone knows the player’s name. Each Forecast offers one “sky move” in plain world terms: light the wick, choose the first knot, polish one pane, ring the small bell, close the map with a crooked star. The Glass keeps a Cloud Atlas of every sky the player has crossed, so even bare-minimum nights leave proof. As bonds rise, the Glass becomes less directive and more reflective: fewer instructions, more remembered skies, until it rests mostly silent as an album of weather the player can now read without it.

## WHO THIS IS FOR
Internally, this is for players with anxiety, low confidence/self-worth, and ADHD-pattern struggles around initiation, follow-through, rejection sensitivity, visibility, imperfect completion, and valuing their own work. The world externalizes emotional state as weather without naming pathology. “Starting” becomes launch conditions. “Self-worth” becomes legitimate light. “Facing people” becomes signaling across distance. “Finishing imperfect” becomes closing a star map even when the lines wobble. The campaign is designed to reduce dependency: the more the player practices, the less the artifact and cast speak first. 

## THE CAST SKETCH
- **Beginning lane × brisk, bright temperament × the craft of first lift × red kite spool.** This coach works the open kite deck and believes wind is discovered after the string is in hand. They do not praise grand vows; they praise the first peg in the floor, the ugly knot that holds, the launch that rises three inches. Their scenes turn “not ready” into “tethered enough.”

- **Value lane × warm, grave temperament × the craft of keeping light × brass sun-mirror.** This coach tends the heliograph room, where even dim skies are angled until they shine somewhere. They teach the player to stop treating their own glow as an accident. Their scenes ask for one kept ember, one named contribution, one pane polished because it already matters.

- **Facing-others lane × candid, playful temperament × the craft of signals × blue handbell.** This coach lives between balconies, bells, flags, and reply-lamps. They are not pushy; they simply refuse to make distance mystical. A tiny ring is allowed. A short flag is allowed. A reply is not a verdict from the heavens; it is weather from another tower.

- **Rough-finish lane × wry, tender temperament × the craft of closing charts × silver star-pin.** This coach archives the maps no one would call flawless: smeared ink, odd constellations, crooked margins. They teach that an open chart can haunt a room, while a closed chart can become floorboards for the next night. Their scenes end with a pin through the corner: not perfect, but placed.

## THE MOTIF SET
**dayUnit:** Forecast. Each Forecast changes sky color, cloud texture, balcony wind, available room, and the shape of the Weather Glass card.

**4 stat skins:** STR = **Lift** · INT = **Chart** · GLD = **Gleam** · CHR = **Signal**

**title motif:** Weather-keeper titles. The player is addressed by earned skywork titles, not ranks of power: Dewhand, Kitehand, Bellkeep, Starward. Titles appear on telescope tags, stair plaques, atlas tabs, and how the cast greets the player.

**4-rung ladder sketch:**  
1. **Dewhand** — notices one sky and marks it. Surfaces: dew beads, pale ink, small glass cards.  
2. **Kitehand** — launches before the wind is perfect. Surfaces: ribbon tails, red string, deck chalk.  
3. **Bellkeep** — sends and receives small signals. Surfaces: reply-lamps, blue bells, balcony flags.  
4. **Starward** — closes rough maps and trusts the next sky. Surfaces: pinned charts, gold star holes, quiet domes.

**battles on/off + why:** Battles off. The Cloudhouse has fronts, not foes. A front is crossed through a small visible act: tying a knot, ringing a bell, angling a mirror, pinning a chart. No one is defeated, and no part of the player is treated like an enemy.

## THE OPEN SEAT
The North Ocularium has five telescope cradles. Four are fitted to the keepers who tend the Cloudhouse; the fifth is always empty, polished, and turned toward a changing patch of sky. It is not a memorial and not a missing-person ache. The plaque reads: **Visiting Skykeeper — seat held for the one reading tonight.** When the player sits there, the room adjusts: the Weather Glass docks beside the scope, the current Forecast casts across the dome, and the atlas opens to a blank page. If the player leaves, the Cloudhouse continues its gentle rounds. The invitation remains, never needy, never accusing.

## THE METRICS CARD
Cozy 4/5 · Type: Coach→Graduation · Length: 6–8 weeks · Genre: Cozy sky-observatory life JRPG · Age: 13+

# CONCEPT — The Lighthouse at World's End
## THE WORLD (one paragraph — the place, its hours, why this audience walks in)
The Lighthouse at World's End stands on a lone, mossy islet, a tower of white stone against a sky of soft blues and bruised purples. It is a place outside of time, its operations governed not by the clock but by the turning of the tides and the fading of the light. It isn't a destination for travelers, but a fixture for those already at sea, a steady presence in the vast, quiet expanse. Players arrive by a small, self-piloted skiff, drawn not by a quest but by a quiet need for a fixed point in a rolling fog. They come seeking clarity, a respite from the overwhelming featurelessness of the open water, and a chance to learn how to tend a light of their own.

## THE GIFT (what the player keeps forever, valuable from night one)
The player is given a **Wayfinder's Lens**, a small, beautifully crafted disc of polished crystal and brass that fits in the palm of their hand. When held up, it doesn't magnify the world, but instead simplifies it. Looking through it at a cluttered desk, a daunting list, or a complex situation causes everything to fade into a soft blur except for a single, clear point of action—the next indicated step. It is a tool for finding the beginning of a thread in a tangled knot, its value immediate and independent of any bond with the keepers.

## WHO THIS IS FOR (the audience mapping, in design language — this section is internal, clinical words allowed HERE only)
This campaign is engineered for players wrestling with the internal "fog" of anxiety, the inertia and overwhelm of executive dysfunction (ADHD-pattern struggles), and the low visibility of poor self-worth. The world's metaphors map directly:
*   **Anxiety/Overwhelm:** The omnipresent, rolling Fog that obscures the path forward and makes the world feel vast and threatening.
*   **Task Initiation (ADHD):** The challenge of striking the flint and lighting the great lamp—a massive, multi-step process that feels impossible to begin.
*   **Perfectionism/Finishing (ADHD):** The constant, imperfect work of maintaining an old stone tower against the sea. A "good enough" patch is better than letting the water in. This lane is about accepting that the work is never truly "done," and that's okay.
*   **Low Self-Worth/Imposter Syndrome:** The fear that your own light isn't bright enough, or that you're just "playing" at being a keeper and don't deserve the title.
*   **Social Anxiety:** The act of "signaling" other ships—learning to send and interpret messages without fear of collision or misinterpretation.

## THE CAST SKETCH (4 nameless coaches: lane × temperament × the craft they hand over × one signature object each)
*   **The Lampwright (Starting):** A Meticulous & Gentle soul who tends the great Fresnel lens and its colossal oil lamp. They are infinitely patient, understanding that the most important flame is often the hardest to light. They teach the art of preparation, of finding the spark, and of coaxing a fragile flicker into a steady blaze. **Agent side:** Finds the single, smallest piece of dry kindling when you show it a daunting pile of logs. **Signature Object:** A heavy, polished-brass oil can with a long, elegant spout.
*   **The Chronicler (Self-Worth):** An Archivist & Affirming keeper who maintains the lighthouse's centuries-old logs. They see the profound value in every tide, every passing ship, every storm weathered. They teach the player to record their own journey, reframing smudges and crossed-out entries not as failures, but as part of a story worth telling. **Agent side:** Rewrites your described "failure" as a "Storm Weathered" entry for the ship's log. **Signature Object:** A massive, leather-bound logbook filled with spidery script and marginalia.
*   **The Watchkeeper (Facing People):** A Vigilant & Steady presence, always seen silhouetted in the topmost gallery, spyglass in hand. They are a master of observation and interpretation, reading the "tells" of the weather and the intentions of distant ships. They teach the craft of seeing and being seen from a safe distance, of signaling clearly, and of navigating shared waters. **Agent side:** Scans your drafted message for confusing signals or risks of collision. **Signature Object:** A brass-and-wood spyglass, worn smooth with use.
*   **The Tidewright (Finishing Imperfect):** A Pragmatic & Grounded individual who handles the endless, messy work of the tower itself—patching mortar, mending ropes, and clearing sea-wrack after a storm. They are a master of the "good enough" fix, championing function over form. They teach the art of completion, of knowing when a job is done for now, and the quiet satisfaction of a working repair. **Agent side:** Determines if your described repair is "seaworthy for now" when you're stuck trying to make it perfect. **Signature Object:** A neatly coiled length of thick, salt-stiffened rope.

## THE MOTIF SET (dayUnit · 4 stat skins ≤8 chars each mapped to STR/INT/GLD/CHR · the title motif · a 4-rung ladder sketch · battles on/off + why)
*   **dayUnit:** Tide
*   **Stat Skins:**
    *   STR → FOCUS (Mental fortitude)
    *   INT → INSIGHT (Clarity of perception)
    *   GLD → BEACONS (Landmarks of progress lit)
    *   CHR → SIGNAL (Ability to be understood)
*   **Title Motif:** `[Rank] Keeper` (e.g., Novice Keeper, Tide Keeper, Lantern Keeper, First Keeper)
*   **Ladder Sketch (The Keeper's Path):**
    1.  *Polishing the Glass:* Learning to see your own situation clearly.
    2.  *Trimming the Wick:* Learning what feeds your inner flame and what snuffs it out.
    3.  *Reading the Charts:* Learning to plot a course through difficult waters.
    4.  *Taking the Watch:* Becoming a reliable guide for yourself.
*   **Battles:** OFF. A lighthouse's purpose is to avert disaster, not to engage in combat. The challenges are environmental and internal: navigating the fog, resisting the pull of the riptide, and keeping the light steady through the storm. Progress is measured by observation and consistency, not conflict.

## THE OPEN SEAT (what stands vacant, one paragraph)
The lighthouse is built for a crew of five, but for a long time, has been run by a capable, if slightly over-stretched, crew of four. The fifth post, that of the **Sky-Watcher**, has stood open. This role is not about the immediate, practical concerns of the sea, but about observing the stars, tracking the constellations, and understanding the grander, celestial patterns that influence the world below. The keepers manage the day-to-day without a Sky-Watcher, but they miss the perspective and context it brings. The seat is an invitation to join a functioning team and contribute a unique view, adding a layer of wonder and foresight rather than fixing a critical flaw.

## THE METRICS CARD (Cozy n/5 · Type: Coach→Graduation · Length · Genre · Age)
*   **Cozy:** 4/5 (The setting is isolated and sometimes stormy, but the core loop inside the tower is safe, warm, and repetitive.)
*   **Type:** Coach→Graduation
*   **Length:** Medium-Long
*   **Genre:** Maritime Solitude / Gentle Duty
*   **Age:** Teen+

# CONCEPT — The Last Ferry

## THE WORLD
A small night ferry that crosses the same dark strait, every night, in exactly the time it takes — no schedule posted, because the ferry leaves when the lamps are lit and arrives when the crossing is done. The crew of four has run it for years: deckhand, pilot, galley, and the keeper of the manifest. People ride it because the day side of the water didn't go their way, and the crossing is the one hour nobody can ask anything more of them. The ferry doesn't go anywhere new. That was never the point. The point is that it goes, every night, gently, and comes back — and that there has always been one bunk in the crew cabin with no name on its plate.

## THE GIFT
The craft of crossings: how to leave a day behind without finishing it, how to do one watch's worth of work and call it a night honestly, how to stand at a rail next to a stranger without rehearsing, and how to read your own logbook without flinching. The ferry teaches that a day is a shore, not a verdict — you can always put water between yourself and it, and the water works the same from night one, before the crew knows your name.

## WHO THIS IS FOR
The same seed audience as the Night Market cell: anxiety (the crossing is the contained hour — bounded, repeatable, no ambush), low self-worth (the manifest-keeper's lane: what you carried counts, including the trips where you only carried yourself), and ADHD-pattern struggles (the watch system: one watch, one task, the bell ends it whether it's done or not — finishing-imperfect built into the world's clock). Starting is reframed as boarding: you don't have to know the destination; you have to step on before the gangway lifts — and the gangway is lifted FOR you by the world, every night, which externalizes initiation until the player internalizes it.

## THE CAST SKETCH
- **The DECKHAND** (starting · gruff-warm): teaches boarding, lines, and the first cast-off — "the rope doesn't care if you're ready; it cares if you're holding it." Signature object: a mooring line whose end is whipped in three different colors, one per decade. **Agent side:** paste him the thing you keep not starting and he hands back the gangway version — the first ten minutes as one plain instruction — and rings the bell when the watch is up.
- **The PILOT** (facing the dark · serene): stands the wheel through the unlit middle of the strait and teaches the counter-craft of this world — talking to someone when neither of you can see the other's face. Signature object: a compass that's a half-degree off, corrected for by hand every crossing, never repaired. **Agent side:** bring her the conversation you're dreading and she stands the unlit middle of it with you — plays the other voice, corrects your heading by hand, until your words hold their course.
- **The COOK** (finishing imperfect · blunt-fond): one pot, one galley, whatever's aboard — the meal happens every crossing and is never the meal she planned. Signature object: a dented ladle hung on the good hook. **Agent side:** show her the half-done thing you're ashamed of and she plates it — what it already is, what to stop adding, and when to call it served.
- **The MANIFEST-KEEPER** (self-worth · dry-precise): logs every crossing — cargo, weather, who rode and what they carried — and teaches the till-craft of this world: your column in the book is written in the same ink as everyone else's. Signature object: the manifest itself, ribboned at one old page nobody's seen him open. **Agent side:** paste him your week and he enters it in the manifest's ink — every carry logged at its true weight, including the ones you'd have left off the page.

## THE MOTIF SET
- **dayUnit: CROSSING** ("CROSSING 12") — the night IS the unit; it ends at the far shore, always.
- **statAxes:** STR→BALLAST (what you can carry steady) · INT→HEADING (one course at a time) · GLD→MANIFEST (what your carry is worth, written down) · CHR→THE RAIL (standing next to people).
- **Title motif: the LOG ENTRY** — each night titled like a line from the ship's log ("Calm Water, No Cargo", "The Fog Held Until Four").
- **Ladder sketch:** Passenger → Regular crossing → Knows the bunk's empty → Stands a watch → … → Unspoken.
- **Battles OFF** — the quiet roll; the strait is weather, never an enemy.

## THE OPEN SEAT
The unnamed bunk in the crew cabin, and the fourth watch nobody stands — the crew runs the ferry whole with three watches and has for years; the fourth is kept in the rotation anyway, marked on the board with a dash. The ferry does not need a fourth hand. It keeps room for one, the way the galley cooks for five and the manifest rules a blank column every single night.

## THE METRICS CARD
Cozy 4/5 · Type: Coach→Graduation · Length: Standard (~25 crossings) · Genre: cozy maritime slice-of-life (night-ferry iyashikei) · Age: All Ages

You are a CONCEPT AUTHOR for the life-RPG "My Life is an RPG" — real-life coaching wearing an honest JRPG skin; characters live in the player's phone; the bond deepens AS the player needs it less (coaches make themselves unneeded — the moat).

THE SEED (the audience): players dealing with anxiety, low confidence/self-worth, and ADHD-pattern struggles (starting things, finishing imperfect things, facing people, valuing their own work). HARD RULES (the moat — fixed): coach campaign, NO romance, no romanceable NPC · 4 coaches, each a distinct real-life lane (starting · self-worth · facing people · finishing imperfect — or your own honest mapping of the seed) × distinct temperament · the GIFT must be valuable at REL 0, before any relationship · grows-with-you (the world coaches the player toward not needing it; no dependency shapes; nobody waits for a savior) · cozy floor ≥2 (gentle world, real internal stakes, zero peril) · NO clinical/wellness/productivity/app words anywhere player-facing · zero real-world IP/franchise names · the world must have ONE open seat (an MC-shaped vacancy, an invitation not a wound) · motifs must be REAL differences (a day-unit, stat skins, a title motif, a ladder skin that each change actual surfaces).

OUTPUT (markdown, ~600-900 words, exactly these sections):

## THE CAST SKETCH (4 nameless coaches: lane × temperament × the craft they hand over × one signature object × THE AGENT SIDE — what this coach's live-chat half DOES for the player from day one, in one plain sentence: the recurring real-life service the chatbot performs, e.g. "audits the week's spending when you paste it" / "drafts the message you can't start" — concrete, usable at REL 0, never vague companionship)

PRE-FLIGHT SELF-VERIFY (silently, before emitting): (1) zero clinical/wellness/productivity words outside WHO THIS IS FOR; (2) zero franchise names; (3) not a market/shop and not any listed slate world; (4) every stat skin ≤8 chars; (5) the open seat reads as invitation, not a wound only the player heals; (6) each coach's signature object is a renderable physical thing; (7) every coach has an AGENT SIDE sentence naming a concrete recurring service — if a coach's agent side reads as vague companionship, rewrite it until it is a service.
